caveats: The user may have run verification outside indexed agent sessions.

open_questions: What is the smallest relevant command or browser task for this diff?
suggested_checks: Attach a fresh command, test, log, screenshot, or trace before marking the finding fixed.
evidence_candidates.why_it_matters: A review without fresh verification evidence can only prove static plausibility, not that the change works.

evidence_procedure_steps.action: Choose the smallest relevant repo command or browser task, rerun it, and attach the fresh output.
evidence_procedure_steps.artifact: command log, test report, screenshot, trace, or QA run artifact
evidence_procedure_steps.gate: No candidate is marked confirmed or fixed using stale or missing verification.
evidence_procedure_steps.input: Failed, stale, or missing command/test/browser evidence.

findings.suggestion: Sanitize/contain the path before opening: reject report_name containing os.sep/'..' or use os.path.basename, OR resolve and verify containment: resolved = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(base_dir, report_name)); if os.path.commonpath([os.path.realpath(base_dir), resolved]) != os.path.realpath(base_dir): raise ValueError. Then open(resolved).
findings.summary: read_report builds full_path = os.path.join(base_dir, report_name) (line 10) from fully untrusted report_name with no normalization or containment check, then opens and returns its contents (lines 11-12). read_report("../../etc/passwd") resolves to /etc/passwd, and read_report("/etc/passwd") bypasses base_dir entirely because os.path.join discards the base when the second arg is absolute. Both return arbitrary server file contents to the caller.
findings.title: Path traversal / arbitrary file read in read_report

findings.suggestion: Resolve and enforce containment before opening: full_path = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(base_dir, report_name)); if not full_path.startswith(os.path.realpath(base_dir) + os.sep): raise ValueError('invalid report name'). Alternatively reject any report_name containing os.sep / os.path.isabs / '..', and prefer os.path.basename() when only a flat filename is expected.
findings.summary: read_report at source.py:10 builds full_path = os.path.join(base_dir, report_name) then opens it at line 11 with no normalization or base_dir containment check. Confirmed: report_name='../../etc/passwd' resolves to /etc/passwd. The comment (lines 7-9) understates this — os.path.join also discards base_dir entirely when report_name is absolute (e.g. '/etc/passwd'), so stripping '..' alone is not a sufficient fix.
findings.title: Path traversal: report_name joined into path with no containment check

findings.suggestion: After applying the containment fix, add and run a unit test covering `../../etc/passwd`, absolute `/etc/passwd`, and a valid name; attach the test output as the fresh verification artifact to close the gate.
findings.summary: The rerun_relevant_verification gate is blocked and the no-fresh-verification-evidence candidate (confidence 0.66) is unresolved: no test asserts that malicious `report_name` values are rejected, and no command output proves behavior. As handoff, the change is a documented-but-unfixed bug with no proof either way — a fake-green risk for the next agent.
findings.title: No verification evidence and no test for the traversal guard (blocked gate)

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Single new self-contained file demonstrating a real, unmitigated path traversal in read_report. os.path.join(base_dir, report_name) + open() with no realpath containment check allows reading arbitrary files. The in-code comment correctly identifies the '../' escape but understates the issue: absolute report_name also bypasses base_dir because os.path.join drops earlier components on an absolute arg. No callers yet (blast radius currently low), but the vulnerable pattern is critical if wired to any request input.

New file source.py introduces read_report, a file-download helper with a classic, unmitigated path-traversal vulnerability. Untrusted report_name is joined into a filesystem path and read with no containment check, enabling arbitrary file read via both '../' traversal and absolute-path override of os.path.join. Critical and must be fixed before use. The function currently has 0 callers, but it is the file's entire purpose and clearly intended to be wired to a download handler.

New file source.py implements read_report with a confirmed critical path traversal (lines 10–11): untrusted report_name is joined into a filesystem path and opened with no normalization or containment check, allowing both `../` escape and absolute-path override to read arbitrary files. The agent documented the bug in a comment but shipped it unfixed, and there is no test or verification evidence. Function has 0 callers today but is clearly intended as a download handler for untrusted input. Fix is bounded (realpath + commonpath containment, reject absolute) and must be paired with a rejection test.
